intro: The most concrete split between Bitwarden and 1Password is the pricing model: Bitwarden runs on freemium with paid plans from $1.65/Month, while 1Password uses subscription from $2.99, with a free tier. That shapes how predictable your monthly spend is and how much of the cost sits in infrastructure versus per-seat or per-event fees. Beyond the headline, Bitwarden carries low lock-in (oss with self-host and export) and freemium with paid plans from $1.65/Month; 1Password carries medium lock-in (items exportable to 1pux/json but deeply integrated workflows) and subscription from $2.99, with a free tier. Bitwarden is a fit when you are teams. 1Password is a fit when you are teams. The honest trade-off: Bitwarden is explicitly not for solo, static projects; 1Password is explicitly not for solo, static projects. On the product side, Bitwarden is described as Open-source password and secrets manager with end-to-end encryption, SSO support, and self-hosting options, and 1Password is described as Secrets Management tool for developers.
quick_take: Bitwarden is for teams; 1Password is for teams; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Bitwarden if its freemium pricing model fits your budget shape better than 1Password's subscription model, and you are teams.
verdict_b: Choose 1Password if its subscription pricing model fits your budget shape better than Bitwarden's freemium model, and you are teams.

faq.a: Bitwarden starts at $1.65/Month; 1Password starts at $2.99. On entry price alone that is the comparison, but real cost scales with users, events or volume, which the entry prices do not capture. Check each vendor's full tier list before committing.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Bitwarden to 1Password?
faq.a: Migration from Bitwarden to 1Password is plausible because Bitwarden has low lock-in (oss with self-host and export). You would still have to map config, permissions and integrations to 1Password's model. Plan for dual-run and rollback.

On our scoring Bitwarden rates 5/5 for developer experience versus 4/5 for 1Password. That suggests day-to-day workflows (CLI, SDKs, docs) are smoother in Bitwarden. Scores are directional; run a short spike on your own stack to confirm.

faq.q: Is 1Password a good alternative to Bitwarden?
faq.a: 1Password targets the same best-fit audience as Bitwarden (teams). Note that 1Password is explicitly not for solo, static projects, so it only replaces Bitwarden if you are outside that exclusion. The lock-in profile differs (low vs medium), which is the main thing to weigh.
